(ہندکو, romanised: *Hindko*, IPA: [[ˈɦɪndkoː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu")) is a cover term for a diverse group of [Lahnda](/wiki/Lahnda "Lahnda") dialects spoken by several million people of various ethnic backgrounds in several areas in northwestern [Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan"), primarily in the province of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and northwestern regions of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan").[[2]](#cite_note-3)

A Hindko speaker.

**Hindko** (ہندکو, romanised: *Hindko*, IPA: [[ˈɦɪndkoː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Hindi_and_Urdu "Help:IPA/Hindi and Urdu")) is a cover term for a diverse group of [Lahnda](/wiki/Lahnda "Lahnda") dialects spoken by several million people of various ethnic backgrounds in several areas in northwestern [Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan"), primarily in the province of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and northwestern regions of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan").[[2]](#cite_note-3)

The name "Hindko" means "the Indian language" or "language of Hind",[[b]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980482-8)[[3]](#cite_note-Venkatesh2019-4)[[7]](#cite_note-Jaffrelot2004-9)[[4]](#cite_note-West2010-5) and refers to the Indo-Aryan speech forms spoken in the northern Indian subcontinent,[[3]](#cite_note-Venkatesh2019-4)[[8]](#cite_note-GeijbelsAddleton-10)[[5]](#cite_note-Sumra1992-6) in contrast to the neighbouring [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto"), an [Iranic language](/wiki/Iranian_language "Iranian language") spoken by the Pashtun people.[[4]](#cite_note-West2010-5)[[8]](#cite_note-GeijbelsAddleton-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) An alternative local name for this language group is *Hindki*.[[c]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch19924-13) A speaker of Hindko may be referred to as *Hindki*, *Hindkun*, or *[Hindkowan](/wiki/Hindkowan "Hindkowan")* (*Hindkuwan*).[[11]](#cite_note-14)

Like other Lahnda varieties, Hindko is derived from the [Shauraseni Prakrit](/wiki/Shauraseni_Prakrit "Shauraseni Prakrit").[[12]](#cite_note-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16) Hindko to some extent is mutually intelligible with [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") and [Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_language "Saraiki language"),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERahman1996211-17) and has more affinities with the latter than with the former.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1979200–1-18)

There is a nascent language movement,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1979198-19) and in recent decades Hindko-speaking intellectuals have started promoting the view of Hindko as a separate language.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERahman1996211-17) There is a literary tradition based on **Peshawari**,[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980486,_497,_509-20) the urban variety of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") in the northwest, and another one based on the language of [Abbottabad](/wiki/Abbottabad "Abbottabad") in the northeast.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERahman1996211–14-21) In the 2023 census of Pakistan, 5.6 million people declared their language to be Hindko,[[19]](#cite_note-2023_census2-22) while a 2020 estimate placed the number of speakers at 7 million.[[1]](#cite_note-e26hno-2)

## Geographic distribution and dialects

Varieties of Hindko are primarily spoken in a core area in the district of [Attock](/wiki/Attock_District "Attock District") in the northwestern corner of the province of Punjab, and in two neighbouring regions: in Peshawar to the north-west, and [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") to the north-east, both in the province of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (formerly known as the North-West Frontier Province). The Hindko of Hazara also extends east into nearby regions of [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir").

The central dialect group comprises [Kohati](/wiki/Kohati "Kohati") (spoken in the city of [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat "Kohat") and a few neighbouring villages in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa) and the three closely related dialects of Attock District, Punjab: [Chacchi](/wiki/Chacchi "Chacchi") (spoken in [Attock](/wiki/Attock_Tehsil "Attock Tehsil") and [Haripur Tehsils](/wiki/Haripur_Tehsil "Haripur Tehsil")), [Ghebi](/wiki/Ghebi "Ghebi") (spoken to the south in [Pindi Gheb Tehsil](/wiki/Pindi_Gheb_Tehsil "Pindi Gheb Tehsil")) and [Awankari](/wiki/Awankari_dialect "Awankari dialect") (spoken in [Talagang Tehsil](/wiki/Talagang_Tehsil "Talagang Tehsil"), now part of [Chakwal District](/wiki/Chakwal_District "Chakwal District")).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980484–86-23)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch199257,_85-24) Rensch's classification based on lexical similarity[[d]](#cite_note-25) also assigns to this group the rural dialects of [Peshawar District](/wiki/Peshawar_District "Peshawar District").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch199255–56-26) Shackle, however, sees most[[e]](#cite_note-27) of them as closely related to the urban variety of Peshawar City.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980497–98-28)

In a group of its own is Peshawari,[[f]](#cite_note-29) the [prestigious](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") urban variety spoken in the city of Peshawar and the one that is promoted as a standardised literary language.[[24]](#cite_note-30) It has a wide dialectal base[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980497-31) and has undergone the influence of [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and [Standard Punjabi](/wiki/Standard_Punjabi "Standard Punjabi").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch199255–56-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980509-32)

A separate group is formed in the northeast by the relatively homogeneous dialects of the [Hazara region](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region"),[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980485-33)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch199256-34) which are collectively known as *Hazara Hindko* or *Northern Hindko*, with the variety spoken in [Kaghan Valley](/wiki/Kaghan_Valley "Kaghan Valley") known as *Kaghani*,[[1]](#cite_note-e26hno-2) and the variety of [Tanawal](/wiki/Tanawal_Tehsil "Tanawal Tehsil") known variously as *Tanoli Hindko*, *Tanoli* or *Tinauli*.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawaz20141–4-35)
Hindko is also spoken further east into Kashmir. It is the predominant language of the [Neelum Valley](/wiki/Neelum_Valley "Neelum Valley"), in the north of Pakistan-administered Azad Kashmir, where it is locally known as *Parmi* (or *Pārim*; the name likely originated in the Kashmiri word *apārim* 'from the other side', which was the term used by the Kashmiris of the [Vale of Kashmir](/wiki/Vale_of_Kashmir "Vale of Kashmir") to refer to the highlanders, who spoke this language).[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkhtarRehman200768–69-36) This variety is also spoken across the [Line of Control](/wiki/Line_of_Control "Line of Control") into Indian-administered [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESohailRehmanKiani2016-37)

The whole dialect continuum of Hindko is partitioned by *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* into two languages: Northern Hindko ([ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3") code: hno)[[1]](#cite_note-e26hno-2) for the dialects of Hazara, and Southern Hindko (ISO 639-3: hnd)[[32]](#cite_note-e26hnd-38) for the remaining varieties. This grouping finds support in the results of the intelligibility testing done by Rensch, which also found out that the southern dialects are more widely understood throughout the Hindko area than are the northern ones.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch199258–62-39)

Hindko dialects gradually transition into other varieties of Lahnda and Punjabi to the south. For example, to the southwest across the [Salt Range](/wiki/Salt_Range "Salt Range") are found dialects of Saraiki,[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980484-40) and at least one of these – the one spoken in the [Dera Ismail Khan District](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District") – is sometimes also referred to as "Hindko".[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch19927–8,_57-41) To the southeast, Hindko is in a dialect continuum with [Pahari–Pothwari](/wiki/Pahari%E2%80%93Pothwari "Pahari–Pothwari"), with the [Galyat](/wiki/Galyat "Galyat") region of Abbottabad district and the area of [Muzaffarabad](/wiki/Muzaffarabad "Muzaffarabad") in Azad Kashmir approximately falling on the boundary between the two.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELothersLothers2010-42)

There are Hindko [diasporas](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") in major urban centres like [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"),[[37]](#cite_note-43) as well as in some neighbouring countries.

Before [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, a substantial population of Hindkowans were [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh"). This population migrated en masse to India.[[8]](#cite_note-GeijbelsAddleton-10)[[38]](#cite_note-DT2003-44) These Hindkowans have completely assimilated into larger Punjabi-speaking and [Hindi-speaking speakers](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") in India, with only few elders identifying as hindokowans.[[39]](#cite_note-KVenkatesh-45)

There was also a small Sikh and Hindu Hindkowans diaspora in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Indians_in_Afghanistan "Indians in Afghanistan"), who became established there during the Sikh Empire in the first half of the 19th century. Most of them emigrated to India or western countries since the war and subsequent rise of the Taliban, and the total population, being not more than 60 (as of 2024).[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWyeth2018-46)

## Social setting

There is no generic name for the speakers of Hindko because they belong to diverse ethnic groups and tend to identify themselves by the larger families or [castes](/wiki/Caste "Caste"). However, the Hindko-speaking community belonging to the [Hazara Division](/wiki/Hazara_Division "Hazara Division") of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa is sometimes recognised collectively as [Hazarewal](/wiki/Hazarewal "Hazarewal"), while the urban settlers in the cities of Peshawar and Kohat are simply known as [Pishoris](/wiki/Peshawaris "Peshawaris") and Kohatis, respectively.[[41]](#cite_note-47) A large number of Hindko speakers in the Hazara Division are Pashtuns.[[42]](#cite_note-:0-48) Some of those speak Hindko as their mother tongue while others as a second language.[[42]](#cite_note-:0-48) These include the [Tahirkhelis](/wiki/Tahirkheli "Tahirkheli"), [Swatis](/wiki/Swati_(Pashtun_tribe) "Swati (Pashtun tribe)"), [Yusufzais](/wiki/Yusufzai "Yusufzai"), [Jadoons](/wiki/Jadoon "Jadoon") and [Tareens](/wiki/Tareen "Tareen").[[42]](#cite_note-:0-48) The other Hindko speakers include the [Sayyids](/wiki/Sayyid "Sayyid"), [Awans](/wiki/Awan_(tribe) "Awan (tribe)"), [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_tribe "Mughal tribe"), [Tanolis](/wiki/Tanoli "Tanoli"), Turks, [Qureshis](/wiki/Qureshi_(surname) "Qureshi (surname)") and [Gujjars](/wiki/Gurjar "Gurjar").[[42]](#cite_note-:0-48)

The most common second language for Hindko-speakers in Pakistan is Urdu and the second most common one is Pashto.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch199280-49) In most Hindko-speaking areas, speakers of Pashto live in the same or neighbouring communities (although this is less true in Abbottabad and Kaghan Valley). The relationship between Hindko and its neighbours is not one of stable bilingualism. In terms of [domains of use](/wiki/Language_domain "Language domain") and number of speakers, Hindko is dominant and growing in the north-east; in Hazara for example, it is displacing Pashto as the language in use among the Swati Pathans,[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERensch19924–5-50) and in the Neelam Valley of Azad Jammu & Kashmir it is gaining ground at the expense of the minority languages like Kashmiri.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkhtarRehman200769-51) In the cities of Kohat and Peshawar, on the other hand, it is Hindko that is in a weaker position. With the exodus of the Hindko-speaking Hindus and Sikhs after Partitition and the consequent influx of Pashtuns into the vacated areas of the urban economy, there have been signs of a shift towards Pashto.[[46]](#cite_note-52)[[47]](#cite_note-GeijbelsAddleton2-53)

Hindko contrasts [stop consonants](/wiki/Stop_consonants "Stop consonants") at the [labial](/wiki/Labial_consonant "Labial consonant"), [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant"), [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant"), [palatal](/wiki/Palatal_consonant "Palatal consonant") and [velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") places of articulation. The [palatals](/wiki/Palatal_consonant "Palatal consonant") have been described as pure stops (/[c](/wiki/Voiceless_palatal_plosive "Voiceless palatal plosive") [ɟ](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_plosive "Voiced palatal plosive")/) in Awankari,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri1963108–9-54) but as [affricates](/wiki/Affricates "Affricates") (/tʃ dʒ/) in the varieties of Hazara.[[49]](#cite_note-55) For the stop consonants of most varieties of Hindko there is a three-way contrast between voiced (b d ɖ dʒ ɡ), voiceless (p t ʈ tʃ k) and [aspirated](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant") (pʰ tʰ ʈʰ tʃʰ kʰ).[[50]](#cite_note-56) Awankari,[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri196321–22,_26-57) Kohati,[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980487,_498-58) and the varieties of Neelum Valley of Kashmir also distinguish [voiced aspirated](/wiki/Voiced_aspirated "Voiced aspirated") stops (bʱ dʱ ɖʱ dʒʱ ɡʱ).[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKianiBukhariAhmedHameed2012-59) The disappearance of the voiced aspirates from most Hindko varieties has been linked to the development of tone (see [below](#Tone)).

Fricatives like /f/, /x/ and /ɣ/ are found in loans (for example from Persian), but also in native words, often as positional allophones of the corresponding stop.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980487-60) Some documented instances include:

* before other consonants in Kohati (/ɑːxdɑː/ 'saying' versus /ɑːkhɑː/ 'said'),
* in the middle or end of words in Peshawari (/nɪɣʊl/ 'swallow (verb)'),[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980487,_499-61)
* word-medially after stressed vowels in Abbottabad Hindko (/deːxɽ̃ɑː/ 'to look'),[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBashirConners201927-62)
* at the ends of words after vowels in the Hindko of Kashmir (/lɪx/ 'write').[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaroon-Ur-Rashid2015b25-63)

Generally, the fricatives can be found in all positions: at the start, the middle, or at the end of the word (Tanoli Hindko: /xrɑːb/ 'spoilt', /ləxxət/ 'small stick', /ʃɑːx/ 'branch'),[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawaz2014149-64) with relatively few exceptions (one being the restriction on word-final /ɦ/ in the Hindko of Kashmir).[[59]](#cite_note-65) The [labio-dental](/wiki/Labio-dental "Labio-dental") has been explicitly described as the fricative /v/ for the Hindko of Kashmir,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaroon-Ur-RashidKhan2014-66) and Tanawal,[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawaz2014150–153-67) but as the [approximant](/wiki/Approximant "Approximant") /[ʋ](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_approximant "Voiced labiodental approximant")/ in Awankari.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri1963-68)

Apart from /m/ and /n/, Hindko dialects distinguish a varying number of other [nasal consonants](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant").
The [retroflex nasal](/wiki/Retroflex_nasal "Retroflex nasal") is overall shorter than the other nasals,[[63]](#cite_note-69) and at least for the Hindko of Abbottabad it has been described as a nasalised [flap](/wiki/Flap_consonant "Flap consonant"): /ɽ̃/.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBashirConners201926-70) For the Hindko of Kashmir it has been asserted to be an allophone of the alveolar nasal /n/,[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaroon-Ur-Rashid2015b28–29-71) but it is phonemic in Awankari[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri1963113–5-72) and Tanoli; in both dialects it can occur in the middle and at the end of a word, as illustrated by the following examples from Tanoli: /tɑːɳɑ̃ː/ 'straight', /mɑːɳ/ 'pride'.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawaz2014128–129-73) The [velar nasal](/wiki/Velar_nasal "Velar nasal") /ŋ/ is phonemic in Tanoli: /bɑːŋ/ 'prayer call', /mɑːŋ/ 'fiancée',[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawaz2014128–30-74) and in the Hindko of Kashmir, and in both cases it is found only in the middle or at the end of the word.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaroon-Ur-Rashid2015b28-75) In the main subdialect of Awankari, the velar nasal is only found before velar stops,[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri1963113–5-72) and similarly, it is not among the phonemes identified for the Hindko of Abbottabad.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBashirConners201922-76)

Hindko varieties have a single [lateral consonant](/wiki/Lateral_consonant "Lateral consonant"): the alveolar /l/, unlike Punjabi, which additionally has a [retroflex lateral](/wiki/Retroflex_lateral "Retroflex lateral") /ɭ/.[[71]](#cite_note-77) The Awankari dialect, as spoken by Muslims (and not Hindus) and described by Bahri in the 1930s, has a distinctive retroflex lateral, which, however, appears to be in [complementary distribution](/wiki/Complementary_distribution "Complementary distribution") with the alveolar lateral.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri1963116–7,_143-78) There are two rhotic sounds in Hindko: an [alveolar trill](/wiki/Alveolar_trill "Alveolar trill") /r/ (with a varying number of vibrations dependent on the phonetic context), and a [retroflex flap](/wiki/Retroflex_flap "Retroflex flap") /ɽ/.[[73]](#cite_note-79)

Hindko has three short vowels /ɪ/, /ʊ/ and /ə/, and six long vowels: /iː/, /eː/, /æː/, /ɑː/, /oː/ and /uː/.
The vowels can be illustrated with the following examples from Tanoli: /tʃɪpp/ 'big stone', /dʊxx/ 'pain', /kəll/ 'yesterday', /biːɽɑː/ 'button', /keː/ 'what', /bæːrɑː/ 'piece of meat', /tɑːr/ 'Sunday', /tʃoːr/ 'thief', /kuːɽɑː/ 'filth'.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENawaz2014199-81)
Length is strongly contrastive and the long vowels are generally twice as long as the corresponding short vowels.[[76]](#cite_note-82)
The Awankari dialect distinguishes between open and close "o" (/poːlɑː/ 'soft' vs. /pɔːlɑː/ 'shoe').[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri196340–46-83)

Varieties of Hindko also possess a number of [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthongs "Diphthongs") (like /ai/). Which of the many (typically around a dozen) overt vowel combinations should be seen as representing an underlying single segment (a diphthong) rather than simply a sequence of two separate underlying vowels, has varied with the analysis used and the dialect studied.[[78]](#cite_note-84)

#### Nasalised vowels

Hindko dialects possess phonemic [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowels "Nasal vowels") (here marked with a tilde above the vowel: ɑ̃). For example, in the Hindko of Azad Kashmir /bɑː/ 'animal disease' contrasts with /bɑ̃ː/ 'arm', and /toːkeː/ 'meat cutters' with /toːkẽː/ 'hindrances'.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaroon-Ur-Rashid2015b94-85) In this variety of Hindko, as well as in the Hindko of Tanawal, there are nasal counterparts for all, or almost all,[[g]](#cite_note-86) of the long vowels, but none for the short vowels.[[80]](#cite_note-87) In Awankari and the Hindko of Abbottabad, on the other hand, there is contrastive nasalisation for short vowels as well: /kʰɪɖɑː/ 'make one play' contrasts with /kʰɪ̃ɖɑ/ 'scatter' (in Awankari),[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri196361-88) /ɡəɖ/ 'mixing' contrasts with /ɡə̃́ɖ/ 'knot').[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBashirConners201944–45-89)
Peshawari and Kohati presumably follow the pattern of Awankari but have historically lost nasalisation from the round vowels (like /u/ or /o/) at the end of the word.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980500-90)

Additionally, vowels get nasalised [allophonically](/wiki/Allophonic "Allophonic") when adjacent to a nasal consonant. In the varieties of Tanawal and Kashmir both long and short vowels can be nasalised in this way, but only if they precede the nasal consonant: [dõːn] 'washing', [bẽːn] 'crying'.[[84]](#cite_note-91) In the Hindko of Abbottabad, a vowel at the end of some words can be nasalised if it follows a nasal consonant.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBashirConners201944-92) In the Awankari dialect, vowels can be allophonically nasalised both before and after a nasal consonant, but in either case the effect will depend on the position of stress (see [Awankari dialect § Vowels](/wiki/Awankari_dialect#Vowels "Awankari dialect") for more details).[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBahri196361-88)

### Tone

Unlike many Indo-Aryan languages, but in common with other Punjabi varieties, Hindko dialects have a system of [pitch accent](/wiki/Pitch_accent "Pitch accent"), which is commonly referred to as *tone*.[[86]](#cite_note-93) In Punjabi, pitch accent has historically arisen out of the loss of voiced aspirates (/bʱ dʱ ɖʱ dʒʱ ɡʱ ɦ/. Thus in [Standard Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language#Standard_Punjabi "Punjabi language"), if a voiced aspirate preceded the stressed vowel, it would lose its aspiration and cause the appearance of a high tone on that vowel: /dʒiːbʱ/ > /dʒíːb/ 'tongue'. If it followed the stressed vowel, then it would lead to a high tone and lose its aspiration and, if word-initial, its voicing: /ɡʱoːɽaː/ > /kòːɽaː/ 'horse'.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBhardwaj201671–72-94) The same pattern has been reported for Hazara Hindko, with a low rising tone after historically voiced aspirates (/kòːɽaː/ 'horse' < /ɡʱoːɽaː/), a high falling tone before historic voiced aspirates (/kóːɽaː/ 'leper' < /kóːɽʱaː/), and level tone elsewhere (/koːɽaː/ 'bitter'). According to preliminary observations on the Hazara Hindko variety of Abbottabad, the low tone is less prominent than in [Majhi Punjabi](/wiki/Majhi_dialect "Majhi dialect"), and a trace of the aspiration is preserved: for example 'horse' would be /k(h)òːɽaː/.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBashirConners201947–48-95)

The variety spoken to the north-east, in Neelam Valley, has preserved voiced aspirates at the start of the word, so presumably the low tone is not established there. However, there are observations of its appearance in the speech of the residents of the main villages along the highway, likely under the influence of Majhi and Hazara Hindko,[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAkhtarRehman200769-51) and it has similarly been reported in the villages on the Indian side.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESohailRehmanKiani2016109-96)

The southern Hindko varieties have similarly developed tone, but only when the voiced aspirate followed the stressed vowel; voiced aspirates preceding the stress have remained unchanged: thus /ʋə́d/ 'more' (< vədʱ), but /dʱiː/ 'daughter'.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle2003593–94-97) This tone is realised as high falling in Kohati[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShackle1980487-60) and the eastern subdialect of Awankari, but as high in the northwestern Awankari subdialect.[[91]](#cite_note-98) Like Kohati, the variety of Peshawar has high falling tone before historic voiced aspirates. However, it has also developed a distinct tone on stressed vowels after historic voiced aspirates, like northern Hindko and Majhi, with a similar loss of aspiration and voicing. But in contrast to Majhi, this tone is also high falling, and it is distinguished by the accompanying [glottalisation](/wiki/Glottalisation "Glottalisation"): /tˀîː/ 'daughter', /vəˈtˀɑ̂ːiː/ 'congratulations'.[[92]](#cite_note-99)

## Alphabet

## Literature

The Gandhara Hindko Board is a leading organisation that has been active in the preservation and promotion of the Hindko and culture since 1993. The board was launched in Peshawar in year 1993 to preserve and promote Hindko —the second most spoken of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province of Pakistan. It brings out four regular publications— *Hindkowan*, *The Gandhara Voice*, " Sarkhail" and "Tarey" and a number of occasional publications. Late professor Zahoor Ahmad Awan of Peshawar city, the author of 61 books and publications, was the founding-chairman of the board. Now the board is headed by Ejaz Ahmad Qureshi. The board has published first Hindko dictionary and several other books on a variety of topics. With head office in Peshawar, the organisation has regional offices in other cities of the province where Hindko is spoken and understood.

In 2003 the Gandhara Hindko Board published first a Hindko dictionary which was compiled by a prominent linguists from Abbottabad, Sultan Sakoon. The board published a second more comprehensive Hindko dictionary in 2007 prepared by Elahi Bakhsh Awan of the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London"). He is the author of Sarzamin e Hindko, and Hindko Sautiyat. His three booklets on Hindko [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") were published by the [University of Peshawar](/wiki/University_of_Peshawar "University of Peshawar") in the late 1970s.

The Idara-e-Faroghe Hindko based in Peshawar is another body that is promoting Hindko. [Riffat Akbar Swati](/wiki/Riffat_Akbar_Swati "Riffat Akbar Swati") and Aurangzeb Ghaznavi are main people of this organisation. The Idara has published the first Hindko translation of the Quran by Haider Zaman Haider and the first Ph.D. thesis on Hindko by E.B.A. Awan. A monthly magazine *Faroogh* is also published regularly from Peshawar under supervision of Aurangzeb Ghaznavi. In Karachi Syed Mehboob is working for the promotion of Hindko. His articles are frequently published in *Farogh* monthly. He is organiser of Hindko Falahi Forum.

Many organisations like Bazm-e-Ilm-o-Fun Abbottabad and Halqa-e-Yaraan Shinkyari promote Hindko and literature. Asif Saqib, Sufi Abdur Rasheed, Fazal-e-Akbar Kamal, Sharif Hussain Shah, Muhammad Farid, Yahya Khalid, Nazir Kasalvi, and Muhammad Hanif have contributed a lot in this regard. Sultan Sakoon has written the First Hindko dictionary that has been published by Gandhara Hindko Board. Sultan Sakoon stands out for his literary contribution as he is a prolific writer and his books including those on Hindko proverbs and Hindko riddles have been published.

### Poetry example

An excerpt from the *Kalām* of [Ahmad Ali Saayein](/wiki/Ahmad_Ali_Saayein "Ahmad Ali Saayein"):[[94]](#cite_note-101)

الف اول ہے عالم ہست سی او   
ہاتف آپ پکاریا بسمہ اللہ
  
فیر قلم نوں حکم نوشت ہویا
  
ہس کے قلم سر ماریا بسمہ اللہ
  
نقشہ لوح محفوظ دے وچ سینے
  
قلم صاف اتاریا بسمہ اللہ
  
اس تحریر نوں پڑھ کے فرشتیاں نے
  
سائیاں شکر گزاریا بسمہ اللہ

**Transliteration:** *Alif-Awal hai Alam e hast sī o*
  
*Hātif āp pukārā Bismillah*
  
*Fīr Qalam nū̃ hukum e Nawišt hoyā*
  
*Hus ke qalam sir māriyā Bismillah*
  
*Naqšā Loh e Mahfūz dai wic sine*
  
*Qalam sāf utāriyā Bismillah*
  
*Is Tahrīr nū̃ paṛah ke Farištiyā̃ ne*
  
*Sāiyā̃ Šukar guzāriyā Bismillah*

**Translation:** "He is the foremost from the world of existence  
Voice of the unseen exclaimed Bismillah
  
The pen was ordered to write
  
Pen carried out the order to write Bismillah
  
When angels read this composition
  
Saaieaan, they showed their thankfulness with Bismillah"

### Proverbs

Hindko has a rich heritage of proverbs (Hindko *matlaan*, sg. *matal*).[[95]](#cite_note-102)[[96]](#cite_note-103) An example of a proverb:

جدھر سر ادھر سرہانڑا

Transliteration: *Jidur sir udur sarhanra*

Translation: "Good person gains respect everywhere."
